utterances: Now, we're joined by NAACP head, Bruce Gordon.
Bruce, we just heard from Marc Morial talking about the importance of Mrs. King.

I want you to address the idea that as we lose these icons and still look at today and the issues that we have, it's very important for the black community not to fall asleep in thinking that things are A-okay.
I think that is the key point.

And one of the observations I make is that there are many of us in America, many black folks, who have reaped many of the benefits of the Coretta Scott King/Martin Luther King movement, and many have been lulled to sleep to think that all is well.
The fact of the matter is that the gap between have's and have not's is getting larger.
Most black folks are on the short side of that equation.

And I think that now more than ever, if we choose to honor Coretta Scott King, then we've got to roll up our sleeves, face the realities, and get recommitted to the civil rights movement, which, in my terms today, is closing the gap in the quality of life between have's and have not's.
Unless we think we've made advances, and we certainly have, there are those bell ringers that always bring us back.
Most recently the NAACP had a fight and just gave a settlement down in South Carolina.
Give us the information on that, Bruce, if you would.
Well, Ed, that's the beginning of the process.
As you well know, I believe every summer, two different groups find their way down to South Carolina.
They are all motorcycle riders, but one group is white and one group is black.
And what we've found is when the black bike riders arrived in Myrtle Beach, many of the establishments normally open to the white riders, closed up when the black folks got to town.

So we got involved in a lawsuit representing them, filing suits both against the municipality itself, but also, what you will continue to hear over time is that we've actually identified each of those establishments that, for whatever reason or another, closed, for instance closed to do inventory that weekend.
We have identified them.
We are in negotiations with them.
And in aggregate, I expect a meaningful settlement, as well as a recognition that we still need to be treated the same way that white folks are.

And in terms of staying vigilant, everyone talking about the Super Bowl, but does take us back to an issue that we covered extensively last week, the idea that so much had been made of making sure that in the higher ranks, African Americans were given opportunity.
We saw a third of the coaching ranks open, no minority hires, no new hires; a lateral shift with Herman Edwards.
There's another issue that is raising the ire of many this week as they revel in the Super Bowl pomp and circumstance.
What's disappointing about that to me is if you look at the performance of black coaches this year, they were exceptional.
They brought teams back in Cincinnati, in Indianapolis.
We've got coaches, for instance, who have had superb records.
This is not a matter of a numbers game.
This is not a matter of giving black coaches a chance.
This is a matter of the fact that black coaches have earned the right to lead teams and lead teams to victory and generate profits for those franchises.
So it's just not clear to me why team owners don't want to take advantage of the coaching talent that these black coaches can bring to the table.
Bruce, what's on the horizon?
What's the organization working on?
And what should we be looking to that perhaps has not made the radar, not in headlines yet?
Mr.
B.
GORDON: Well, I'm actually in Gulfport, Miss.
today.
We've formed an alliance that we're going to announce today with Habitat for the Humanity.
This is an organization that builds 15,000 homes a year and creates, therefore, 15,000 new homeowners.

We're going to work with them to make sure that are opportunities find their way to people in our community, and given the effects of Katrina in the Gulfport area, we are about to break ground on some new homes in a historic community, Forest Heights, that was built over 30 years ago by Dorothy Height and the National Council of Negro Women, giving poor folks a chance to own a home, and those homes were destroyed.
We're in the process of rebuilding them.
So that's the post-Katrina project continues to move forward.

But, in addition to that, as you well heard in the State of the Union, the President spoke to Katrina, but it didn't enjoy the center stage position that things like the domestic surveillance program did, so we need to vigilant and continue to focus on programs that are going to restore some order and some economic stability in the Gulf region, and you can expect the NAACP to be front and center.
Bruce Gordon, we're excited about having you and Mark Morial once a month on the program to give us not only insight but issues that your organizations are working on.
Look forward to this being the start of something good, and we appreciate your time.
Mr.
B.
GORDON: Ed, thank you.
Again, we're pleased to announce that Urban League president Mark Morial and NAACP head Bruce Gordon will join us the first Monday of every month.
January marks the beginning of a new year, but it's also the start of a new semester for college and high school students.
Now's the time many minds turn back to the books, and also to money matters about how to pay for those studies.
If you're in the market for free money for college, commentator Kristal Brent Zook says there's plenty out there.
You just have to know where to look.
The U.S.
Education Department has more than $4 billion in stimulus money that its prepared to hand out.
The White House says the Race to the Top fund will set off a wave of innovation by rewarding the best ideas for improving student achievement.
That's what the White House says.
But before a dollar has been awarded, the fund is controversial.
NPR's Larry Abramson reports.
Physics students know this: A good way to move a big, heavy object is with a lever.
If the big object is the U.S.
education system, the lever is money, as President Obama explained last week to an audience at a Wisconsin middle school.

If youre willing to hold yourselves more accountable and if you develop a strong plan to improve the quality of education in your state, then we'll offer you a big grant to help you make that plan a reality.
So the administration is delighted that many states have already rushed to change laws just to make themselves eligible for Race to the Top money.
But, in fact, it's that rush to qualify that makes John Ashley of the Wisconsin Association of School Boards very uneasy.
We now see states scrambling to read the minds of the Department of Education as to what would make their state eligible for the funds.
For example, in Wisconsin, legislators threw out a ban on linking teacher evaluations to student achievement.
The administration said that ban had to go so states could experiment with rewards for teachers whose students do well.
But John Ashley says the new law doesnt really make it any easier to get rid of ineffective teachers.

It seems to be more geared toward helping us reward successful teachers, yet not allowing us to take the information to dismiss a teacher that's not performing effectively, which I think harms the teachers who are doing a great job.
But Education Secretary Arne Duncan says Race to the Top is meant to give states flexibility, not to tell them what to do.
Based upon their history of accomplishments and their capacity to deliver on a courageous plan that they themselves design.
That's in contrast to No Child Left Behind, a legacy of the last administration, which is seen by many local officials as federal meddling in state affairs.
Duncan says Race to the Top will only reward sincere efforts to improve student achievement.
If anyone's just doing things to chase a few dollars, that's not the kind of transformational change we want, and its frankly not the kind of places we're going to invest in.
But many states have invested big time in figuring out just what they need to do to compete for money that is badly needed, given tight school budgets.
The new rules for the competition run well over 100 pages.
The Gates Foundation is handing out grants to help states negotiate the application process.
Anthony Cody coaches science teachers for the Oakland Public Schools, but in his spare time, he's been railing against Race to the Top on his Facebook page.
People with large amounts of money are using that money to leverage change that they want.

Cody and other teachers are particularly worried that Race to the Top will accelerate the reliance on testing that began under No Child Left Behind and that it will punish teachers if scores dont improve.
President Obama campaigned on the notion that we would move away from punishing schools for tests - for low test scores, yet that practice continues.
Now $4 billion is a lot of money, but it's not enough to reward every bright idea.
There will be winners and losers, and most states will probably get nothing from the fund.
But Secretary Arne Duncan says that's not the point.
If folks are doing this because it's the right thing for children, then whether or not they receive the money, children are going to benefit.
The Education Department says Race to the Top is an incentive program.
It's not punitive, the way No Child Left Behind is perceived to be.
But the department will have a lot of public relations work to do to make sure the fund is seen as a helpful lever and not as a big stick.
Larry Abramson, NPR News, Washington.
(Soundbite of music)
This is NPR News.
Unidentified Man #1: All three engines up and burning.
And liftoff.
The final liftoff of Atlantis...
(SOUNDBITE OF SHUTTLE ENGINES)
Unidentified Man #2: Houston now controlling the flight of Atlantis...
The space shuttle will soon be a memory of triumph, sacrifice and underrated achievement.
For the astronauts who flew them, the big old space trucks will be remembered with love.
The very first time I looked back at Earth, it took my breath away.
And you're just up there floating around, completely free to move wherever you want to.
It definitely changes your life.
Astronaut Mike Gernhardt has logged 43 days on the shuttle, including 23 hours and 16 minutes walking in space.
You'd think a 55-year-old astronaut would be satisfied with a cold beer on the back porch, telling about that time he had to take three spacewalks to install the space station airlock.
But, no.

So my dream has always been to go to the Moon and I've worked the last five years tirelessly to develop the architecture, the operations concepts, this new Rover that you might have seen that we actually live in for like two weeks at a time.
And we get into the suits that are hanging off the back on suit ports...
But its cancellation has not thrown Gernhardt for a loop.
Now, instead of going to the Moon, he's working on landing astronauts on a giant passing asteroid.
I'm not one that goes off and cries in the corner.
So, I'm not happy about that and, at the same time, me and my team are attacking, working on the asteroid, very novel ways to anchor and translate on an asteroid surface.
If they cancellation of Constellation doesn't have Gernhardt crying in a corner, others are.
You may have seen the e-mail that pops up from time to time circulated by concerned citizens.
It warns in urgent tones that scholarships and grants for minorities are going unnoticed, free money being ignored.
In some cases, cash is even being returned to funders due to a lack of applications.
Coca-Cola, Xerox and GE are giving away checks; so are Bell Labs, Bill Gates, Boeing and Burger King.
Not everyone is clueless, thank goodness.
About five years ago, a high school student named Shayla Price(ph) typed in the words `minority scholarships' on a Google search.
She came up with a flood of options and quietly spent the next two years filling out some 60 applications.
For the Gates Millennium Scholarship, founded by Bill and Melinda Gates, she had to write over a dozen essays.

It wasn't easy and competition was stiff, but by the time Shayla donned cap and gown at her high school graduation, she had secured over $100,000 in college funds from a total of 13 sources, including the Gates scholarship.
For her unusual claim to fame, Shayla Price made headlines in The New Orleans Times-Picayune just before beginning her studies at Xavier University.
OK, so no one really enjoys asking for handouts, but still, I'm amazed to think that even I survived for over a decade as a freelance writer without so much as glancing at a grant application.
It simply did not occur to me that I could ask for and receive a helping hand.

When I finally did apply for an Alicia Patterson Fellowship last year, at the urging of financially savvy friends, I became one of seven recipients of a year-long grant designed for full-time writers to pursue independent projects of their choosing.
Thanks to this especially generous helping hand, my second book was completed and will be released almost exactly 12 months to the day after receiving my first check.
